comments.body: In Java, List<T> is said to be invariant [in T] -- List<T> cannot be used where List<U> is expected, even if T is a subclass of U or U is a sub-class of T. So even though an ArrayList<String> is a List<String>, a List<ArrayList<String>> is not a List<List<String>>. Although @shmosel's answer does give you a solution in your specific case, I thought you'd be interested in the general cause at play here. See also en.wikipedia.org/wiki/….
It also works with type hint Stream.<List<String>>generate(ArrayList<String>::new) etc.

answers.body:
Seems like the type inference trips up on toList(). It works fine with the old collect() termination:

List<List<String>> lists = Stream.generate(ArrayList<String>::new)
        .limit(n)
        .collect(Collectors.toList());

title: Java Stream API: Incompatible types: List<ArrayList>> cannot be converted to List<List>
body:
My intention was to replace the following snippet:

List<List<String>> lists;
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
    lists.add(new ArrayList<String>());

with Stream API one-liner:

List<List<String>> lists = Stream.generate(ArrayList<String>::new).limit(n).toList();

but I stumbled upon the following error:

error: incompatible types: List<ArrayList<String>> cannot be converted to List<List<String>>

Change of field declaration to:

List<ArrayList<String>>

fixes the error, but I don't really want to break convention to specify only interfaces on lefthand side.

Any ideas what's wrong with the Stream snippet? Or may be suggest your variant.
